title: Feed-Forward Network
description: The per-token layer that refines each token state after attention without mixing information across positions.
openingSummary: A feed-forward network is the per-token layer that follows attention in a transformer block, refining each token state with learned nonlinear processing while leaving token-to-token mixing to attention.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A feed-forward network, often shortened to FFN, is the layer that processes one token state at a time after attention. Attention mixes information across positions first. The feed-forward network then takes each updated token state, applies learned nonlinear processing to that one state, and writes the result back into the residual stream.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: The feed-forward network adds nonlinear capacity after attention has already gathered context. That helps the model turn mixed context into sharper features, patterns, and intermediate abstractions without paying for another token-to-token interaction step.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: Once you recognize the feed-forward slot, many paper claims become easier to decode. A standard dense feed-forward network, a swish gated linear unit, and a mixture-of-experts layer all live in the same place in the block, so a reader can compare them as different ways to spend per-token compute.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token leaves attention and enters one feed-forward design. In the dense baseline, every token uses one shared expand-activate-project path. In a swish gated linear unit, that path splits into value and gate branches before recombining. In a mixture-of-experts layer, a router chooses only a few expert layers for that token. The slot stays the same even when the internal path changes.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: These formulas show a dense feed-forward baseline and a common gated variant. Both act on one token state at a time. The difference is whether the wide hidden path stays single-branch or becomes value-plus-gate.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Nearby feed-forward variants trade dense simplicity, gated expressiveness, and sparse routing. The table below keeps those tradeoffs in one place.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Decoder transformers, encoder stacks, and encoder-decoder systems all include a feed-forward block after attention, even when the exact variant changes.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: The feed-forward network does not mix information across tokens by itself, so it depends on attention to bring the right context into each token state first. Different feed-forward variants also change memory use, routing complexity, and training behavior in different ways.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Many model changes that sound exotic are really swaps inside this one familiar slot. Understanding the feed-forward family helps a reader map new papers back to a stable block-level pattern instead of treating every variant as a brand-new architecture.

computeFlow.alt: Feed-forward network comparison showing a standard dense feed-forward network, a swish gated linear unit, and a mixture-of-experts layer in the same transformer slot
computeFlow.caption: Switch between dense, gated, and sparse feed-forward forms to see what changes inside the same post-attention slot.

comparisonTable.caption: How the main feed-forward variants compare

standard.pathShape: One dense expand -> activate -> project path
standard.activeCompute: All hidden units in one shared feed-forward block
standard.mainStrength: Simple dense baseline with predictable behavior

swiglu.pathShape: Value branch times SiLU gate branch
swiglu.activeCompute: All hidden units plus a learned gate
swiglu.mainStrength: More expressive feature selection inside the same slot

moe.pathShape: Router selects a few expert layers, then merges them
moe.activeCompute: Only top-k experts activate for each token
moe.mainStrength: Adds parameter capacity without activating every expert

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

w1.definition: Projection from model width into the wide hidden layer.

w2.definition: Projection from the wide hidden layer back to model width.

sigma.definition: Pointwise activation applied inside the dense hidden path.

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

wv.definition: Projection that builds the value branch.

wg.definition: Projection that builds the gate branch.

wo.definition: Projection that maps the gated hidden state back to model width.

odot.definition: Elementwise multiplication between the value and gate branches.

sparseNote.label: Sparse path: router sends each token to top-k experts
